title: Syphilis
oneLiner: STI caused by *Treponema pallidum*; four stages (primary, secondary, latent, tertiary) – untreated causes irreversible neuro/cardiovascular damage.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Neurosyphilis (meningovascular):** Acute stroke or aseptic meningitis in a young adult.  
  *Key finding:* cranial nerve palsy (III, VI, VII) + **Argyll Robertson pupil** (accommodates but does not react to light).  
  *Action:* urgent LP + CT/MRI brain; start IV benzylpenicillin immediately.
- **Cardiovascular syphilis (aortic rupture/regurgitation):** Chest/back pain, wide pulse pressure, collapsing pulse, early diastolic murmur.  
  *Key finding:* **CXR shows widened mediastinum** – emergency CT aorta.  
  *Action:* cardiology/surgical review; IV penicillin after blood cultures.
- **Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (within 24h of treatment):** Fever, rigors, hypotension, myalgia.  
  *Key finding:* **occurring after first dose of penicillin** – dangerous in pregnancy (fetal distress) and neurosyphilis (worsening neurology).  
  *Action:* pre-treat with antipyretics; give IV fluids; monitor BP hourly; do NOT stop antibiotics.
sections.Presentation:
- **Primary:** Painless genital ulcer (chancre) + firm, rubbery inguinal lymphadenopathy; heals in 3–6 weeks.
- **Secondary:** Maculopapular rash (palms + soles), flu-like illness, generalised lymphadenopathy, condyloma lata (warty perineal lesions), patchy alopecia, moth-eaten appearance.
- **Latent:** Asymptomatic; early (<2 years from infection) vs late (>2 years).
- **Tertiary:** Gummatous lesions (skin, bone), cardiovascular (aortitis, aortic regurgitation, saccular aneurysm), neurosyphilis (general paresis, tabes dorsalis, meningovascular).
- **Congenital:** Stillbirth, hepatosplenomegaly, rash, rhinitis, osteitis.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Genital ulcer:** Herpes simplex (painful, recurrent), chancroid (painful + purulent), lymphogranuloma venereum (painless + painful buboes).  
  *Splitter:* Painless non-tender ulcer + rubbery nodes = syphilis; painful tender = herpes/chancroid.
- **Rash:** Guttate psoriasis, pityriasis rosea, drug eruption, viral exanthem, HIV seroconversion.
- **Neurological:** Stroke in young – vasculitis (SLE/APS), thrombophilia, HIV. Aseptic meningitis – viral, TB, fungal.
- **HIV co-infection:** Always consider – can alter syphilis presentation (accelerated, multiple chancres).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Neurological exam (pupils, cranial nerves, fundoscopy), vital signs, cardiac auscultation.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFTs, HIV test (mandatory – opt-out), syphilis serology (see below).
- **Imaging:** CXR (widened mediastinum/aortic calcification); Echo if murmur; CT/MRI brain for neurosyphilis.
- **Special tests:**
  - *Syphilis serology:* **Non-treponemal (RPR/VDRL)** – screening & titre monitoring; **Treponemal (TPPA/EIA)** – confirmatory. Both positive = active infection. False-positive RPR in pregnancy, SLE, TB, HIV.
  - *Dark-field microscopy:* Direct visualisation from chancre/lesion (specialist clinic only).
  - *CSF:* For neurosyphilis – VDRL (specific, low sensitivity), cell count (>20/µL), protein, treponemal antibody index.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical + serology. RPR titre >1:8 suggests active infection. CSF VDRL + pleocytosis = neurosyphilis.
- **Never-miss test:** **HIV test** – all patients with syphilis must have HIV serology. Also test for other STIs (chlamydia, gonorrhoea).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If neurological/cardiovascular symptoms: urgent LP + blood cultures → start IV benzylpenicillin (neurosyphilis dose). CT/MRI brain, CT aorta if indicated.
  - If pregnant: treat with benzathine penicillin IM (do NOT delay for test results); monitor for Jarisch-Herxheimer – give paracetamol, IV fluids.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm serology – send urgent RPR + TPPA.
  - Start appropriate penicillin regimen (see Drugs & Doses).
  - HIV co-infection: do not start ART immediately unless CD4 <200 or low (risk of immune reconstitution); discuss with HIV/ID team.
  - Refer to GUM clinic for partner notification (legal duty under UK Health Protection Regulations).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Repeat RPR at 3, 6, 12 months – expect ≥4-fold drop by 6 months. If not falling, consider neurosyphilis or reinfection.
  - Neuro/Ocular/Otic syphilis: repeat LP at 6 months to ensure CSF normalisation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl 1 L over 8h if hypotensive (Jarisch-Herxheimer) – no fixed protocol.
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard LMWH.
  - Diet/NBM: normal unless NBM for LP/surgery.
  - Observations: 4-hourly BP, HR, temp; neuro chart if CNS involvement.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - **Primary, secondary, early latent (<2 years):** Benzathine benzylpenicillin 2.4 MU IM single dose (deep IM).
  - **Late latent (>2 years) or unknown duration:** Benzathine benzylpenicillin 2.4 MU IM weekly × 3 doses.
  - **Neurosyphilis, ocular, otic:** Benzylpenicillin sodium 18–24 MU IV daily in divided doses (e.g., 3–4 MU every 4 hours) for 10–14 days.
  - **Congenital:** Benzylpenicillin IV – dose per BNF children.
- **Alternatives (if penicillin allergy):** Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD for 14 days (early) or 28 days (late). Ceftriaxone 1–2 g IV/IM daily for 10–14 days (neurosyphilis) – caution: cephalosporins may cross-react in severe allergy.
- **Contraindications:** Penicillin allergy – avoid benzylpenicillin; use doxycycline or ceftriaxone.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for penicillin; doxycycline caution in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Using oral penicillin (amoxicillin) – inadequate CSF/tissue levels. Also giving benzathine penicillin IV (IM only). In neurosyphilis, failing to give CSF-penetrating IV benzylpenicillin.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, hypotension, fetal distress), anaphylaxis to penicillin.
- **Chronic:** Neurosyphilis (general paresis, tabes dorsalis, optic atrophy, hearing loss), cardiovascular syphilis

rawMarkdown:
## Syphilis
**One-liner:** STI caused by *Treponema pallidum*; four stages (primary, secondary, latent, tertiary) – untreated causes irreversible neuro/cardiovascular damage.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Neurosyphilis (meningovascular):** Acute stroke or aseptic meningitis in a young adult.  
  *Key finding:* cranial nerve palsy (III, VI, VII) + **Argyll Robertson pupil** (accommodates but does not react to light).  
  *Action:* urgent LP + CT/MRI brain; start IV benzylpenicillin immediately.
- **Cardiovascular syphilis (aortic rupture/regurgitation):** Chest/back pain, wide pulse pressure, collapsing pulse, early diastolic murmur.  
  *Key finding:* **CXR shows widened mediastinum** – emergency CT aorta.  
  *Action:* cardiology/surgical review; IV penicillin after blood cultures.
- **Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (within 24h of treatment):** Fever, rigors, hypotension, myalgia.  
  *Key finding:* **occurring after first dose of penicillin** – dangerous in pregnancy (fetal distress) and neurosyphilis (worsening neurology).  
  *Action:* pre-treat with antipyretics; give IV fluids; monitor BP hourly; do NOT stop antibiotics.

### Presentation
- **Primary:** Painless genital ulcer (chancre) + firm, rubbery inguinal lymphadenopathy; heals in 3–6 weeks.
- **Secondary:** Maculopapular rash (palms + soles), flu-like illness, generalised lymphadenopathy, condyloma lata (warty perineal lesions), patchy alopecia, moth-eaten appearance.
- **Latent:** Asymptomatic; early (<2 years from infection) vs late (>2 years).
- **Tertiary:** Gummatous lesions (skin, bone), cardiovascular (aortitis, aortic regurgitation, saccular aneurysm), neurosyphilis (general paresis, tabes dorsalis, meningovascular).
- **Congenital:** Stillbirth, hepatosplenomegaly, rash, rhinitis, osteitis.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Genital ulcer:** Herpes simplex (painful, recurrent), chancroid (painful + purulent), lymphogranuloma venereum (painless + painful buboes).  
  *Splitter:* Painless non-tender ulcer + rubbery nodes = syphilis; painful tender = herpes/chancroid.
- **Rash:** Guttate psoriasis, pityriasis rosea, drug eruption, viral exanthem, HIV seroconversion.
- **Neurological:** Stroke in young – vasculitis (SLE/APS), thrombophilia, HIV. Aseptic meningitis – viral, TB, fungal.
- **HIV co-infection:** Always consider – can alter syphilis presentation (accelerated, multiple chancres).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Neurological exam (pupils, cranial nerves, fundoscopy), vital signs, cardiac auscultation.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFTs, HIV test (mandatory – opt-out), syphilis serology (see below).
- **Imaging:** CXR (widened mediastinum/aortic calcification); Echo if murmur; CT/MRI brain for neurosyphilis.
- **Special tests:**
  - *Syphilis serology:* **Non-treponemal (RPR/VDRL)** – screening & titre monitoring; **Treponemal (TPPA/EIA)** – confirmatory. Both positive = active infection. False-positive RPR in pregnancy, SLE, TB, HIV.
  - *Dark-field microscopy:* Direct visualisation from chancre/lesion (specialist clinic only).
  - *CSF:* For neurosyphilis – VDRL (specific, low sensitivity), cell count (>20/µL), protein, treponemal antibody index.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical + serology. RPR titre >1:8 suggests active infection. CSF VDRL + pleocytosis = neurosyphilis.
- **Never-miss test:** **HIV test** – all patients with syphilis must have HIV serology. Also test for other STIs (chlamydia, gonorrhoea).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If neurological/cardiovascular symptoms: urgent LP + blood cultures → start IV benzylpenicillin (neurosyphilis dose). CT/MRI brain, CT aorta if indicated.
  - If pregnant: treat with benzathine penicillin IM (do NOT delay for test results); monitor for Jarisch-Herxheimer – give paracetamol, IV fluids.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm serology – send urgent RPR + TPPA.
  - Start appropriate penicillin regimen (see Drugs & Doses).
  - HIV co-infection: do not start ART immediately unless CD4 <200 or low (risk of immune reconstitution); discuss with HIV/ID team.
  - Refer to GUM clinic for partner notification (legal duty under UK Health Protection Regulations).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Repeat RPR at 3, 6, 12 months – expect ≥4-fold drop by 6 months. If not falling, consider neurosyphilis or reinfection.
  - Neuro/Ocular/Otic syphilis: repeat LP at 6 months to ensure CSF normalisation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl 1 L over 8h if hypotensive (Jarisch-Herxheimer) – no fixed protocol.
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard LMWH.
  - Diet/NBM: normal unless NBM for LP/surgery.
  - Observations: 4-hourly BP, HR, temp; neuro chart if CNS involvement.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - **Primary, secondary, early latent (<2 years):** Benzathine benzylpenicillin 2.4 MU IM single dose (deep IM).
  - **Late latent (>2 years) or unknown duration:** Benzathine benzylpenicillin 2.4 MU IM weekly × 3 doses.
  - **Neurosyphilis, ocular, otic:** Benzylpenicillin sodium 18–24 MU IV daily in divided doses (e.g., 3–4 MU every 4 hours) for 10–14 days.
  - **Congenital:** Benzylpenicillin IV – dose per BNF children.
- **Alternatives (if penicillin allergy):** Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD for 14 days (early) or 28 days (late). Ceftriaxone 1–2 g IV/IM daily for 10–14 days (neurosyphilis) – caution: cephalosporins may cross-react in severe allergy.
- **Contraindications:** Penicillin allergy – avoid benzylpenicillin; use doxycycline or ceftriaxone.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for penicillin; doxycycline caution in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Using oral penicillin (amoxicillin) – inadequate CSF/tissue levels. Also giving benzathine penicillin IV (IM only). In neurosyphilis, failing to give CSF-penetrating IV benzylpenicillin.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, hypotension, fetal distress), anaphylaxis to penicillin.
- **Chronic:** Neurosyphilis (general paresis, tabes dorsalis, optic atrophy, hearing loss), cardiovascular syphilis
